# Earth Pulse

> A free, open-source, real-time 3D globe of Earth that runs entirely in the
> browser — no backend and no API keys. It shows live earthquakes, 26 famous
> satellites with clickable orbits plus an optional ~10,700-satellite Starlink
> swarm, the International Space Station, the aurora, real day/night with city
> lights, a full solar-system mode with 11 deep-space probes and the real night
> sky, a continental-drift mode and a Sky AR phone mode. Live at
> https://earth-pulse-rosy.vercel.app/

Earth Pulse turns live public data into a single interactive planet you can
spin, zoom and fly around. It is built with React, TypeScript, Vite and
three.js / globe.gl. All data comes from free, key-less, CORS-friendly public
feeds, so anyone can run it as a static site.

## What it shows (Earth view)

- Live earthquakes from USGS and EMSC, drawn as magnitude-scaled glowing
  ripples, with a 24-hour timeline you can scrub or replay.
- 26 famous satellites (ISS, Tiangong, Hubble, Landsat, Sentinels, GOES…),
  propagated locally every frame with SGP4 from Celestrak TLE data. Click any
  satellite to draw its orbit as a comet-style trail whose bright head marks
  the satellite's current position, and to open its mission card.
- An optional Starlink swarm layer: ~10,700 real Starlink satellites rendered
  as a single GPU-instanced mesh, propagated in a web worker, with real 3D
  models swapping in near the camera.
- The International Space Station, with a live position and a follow camera.
- The real day/night terminator with city lights on the night side.
- A live aurora oval whose reach and brightness follow the NOAA space-weather
  Kp index.
- Latest natural events from NASA EONET: wildfires, storms, volcanoes and ice.
- NASA GIBS "vital signs" imagery layers (true colour, air temperature,
  surface temperature, water vapour, aerosols) with a date playback slider.
- Live Wikipedia edits streaming in real time.

## Modes

- Earth: the live dashboard described above.
- Moon: orbit the real Moon with its true phase and Apollo landing sites.
- Solar system: the Sun, eight planets, the Moon and the major moons on true,
  to-scale orbits, with time-warp to speed up the dance. Every orbit is drawn
  as a comet trail behind its body so you can see where each world is right
  now. It also carries 11 deep-space probes (Voyager 1 & 2, New Horizons,
  Parker Solar Probe, Solar Orbiter, BepiColombo, JUICE, Europa Clipper,
  Psyche, Lucy, Hayabusa2) on their real NASA JPL HORIZONS trajectories, and
  the real night sky: 8,921 naked-eye stars with constellation lines and
  names — click a star for a procedural 3D close-up and, for 13 famous stars,
  a real telescope photo.
- Continental Drift: scrub the globe through 340 million years, from Pangaea
  to today and on to a projected Pangaea Proxima (+250 Myr).
- Sky AR (phones): point the camera at the sky and the overlay identifies the
  satellites passing overhead, plus the Moon and planets, with distances.
- Cinematic kiosk/screensaver tour that starts automatically after a while idle.

## Key facts

- Free and open source (MIT licence).
- No backend, no sign-up, no API keys — a purely static web app.
- Installable as a PWA with an offline service worker.
- Works on desktop, tablet and mobile.
- Bundled data snapshots (satellite TLEs, probe trajectories) are re-baked
  weekly by a GitHub Action so orbits never go stale.
- Data sources: USGS, EMSC, Celestrak, NASA JPL HORIZONS, HYG star database,
  wheretheiss.at, NOAA SWPC, NASA EONET, NASA GIBS, Wikimedia, Scotese/PALEOMAP
  (drift maps). Textures by Solar System Scope (CC BY), zoom imagery © Esri and
  contributors.

## Links

- Live app: https://earth-pulse-rosy.vercel.app/
- Source code: https://github.com/Buggy1111/earth-pulse
